narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school application, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00 and LSAT score of 25.50, while planning to attend full-time. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 90.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam—far too high a risk for the program to approve your admission, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school years 1 and 3, both at the very bottom (1st decile), which stood out starkly against the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your low Year 3 decile had the strongest negative pull, strongly pushing the model toward forecasting bar failure, as it signaled ongoing struggles in a core academic measure tied to bar success. Your LSAT score, well below the average of about 36.7, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, further tilting the prediction against passing. Even your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom rung, added to the model's worries about your early academic foundation compared to peers. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00—slightly under the typical 3.21—actually nudged the prediction a bit toward success, showing some resilience in college-level work. Your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans offered minor positive influences, aligning with the majority of successful students, but they weren't enough to offset the top concerns. Overall, your profile compares unfavorably to typical admitted students, whose stronger deciles and LSATs predict reliable bar passage. While this is disappointing, it's not the end of the road—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year academics through targeted courses or tutoring, and reapplying with an updated profile that addresses these key gaps. Many students in similar situations have succeeded by focusing on these areas, and we're rooting for your next steps.
